CRC 3.1385 ORDER TO SHOW CAUSE
Off Calendar Nov-22-2016 Off Calendar

CRC 3.1385 ORDER TO SHOW CAUSE
Off Calendar Aug-01-2016 Continued To Dec-06-16 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Feb-05-2016 Text Ruling

MASTER CALENDAR JURY
Off Calendar Feb-04-2016 Case Settled - Set For Crc 3.1385 Hrg On Aug-02-16 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION
Off Calendar Jan-08-2016 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances: Melanie Massey of Michael H. Kim, P.C. telephone number 650-697-8899 for Plaintiffs Foo Soo Li and Laudenia Pleitez.
Amy Super of Office of The City Attorney telephone number 415-554-3931 for Defendant.

Ms. Super advised the Court that she has reviewed the proposed order and has no objection. The Court adopted its tentative ruling as follows: DEFENDANTS CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO AND JACK HUBBARD'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION is denied. Defendant fails to maintain its initial burden of production. Defendant does not demonstrate that plaintiffs cannot establish adverse employment actions. In Yanowitz v. L'Oreal USA, Inc. (2005) 36 Cal.4th 1028, 1052-1053, the court addressed the definition of adverse employment action in the context of a retaliation claim. Although an adverse employment action must materially affect the terms, conditions, or privileges of employment to be actionable, the determination of whether a particular action or course of conduct rises to the level of actionable conduct should take into account the unique circumstances of the affected employee as well as the workplace context of the claim' The phrase "terms, conditions, or privileges of employment" evinces a congressional intent "to strike at the entire spectrum of disparate treatment of men and women" in employment,' which includes requiring people to work in a discriminatorily hostile or abusive environment. [Citations.] When the workplace is permeated with 'discriminatory intimidation, ridicule, and insult' [citation] that is 'sufficiently severe or pervasive to alter the conditions of the victim's employment and create an abusive working environment' [citation], Title VII is violated. This standard, which we reaffirm today, takes a middle path between making actionable any conduct that is merely offensive and requiring the conduct to cause a tangible psychological injury. (italics omitted). Yanowitz blurs the traditional notions of harassment and adverse employment action. In other words, an abusive work environment can materially affect the terms and conditions of employment and support a retaliation and discrimination claim. Here, the evidence demonstrates the following: Mr. Li was involved in a work-related accident. Mr. Hubbard ignored his disability restrictions regarding lifting, bending, etc. (Li Depo., 38:25-42:22; 148:21-152:8; Li Dec., par. 4; Hubbard Depo., 113:21-114:7; 117:18-124:5). Mr. Li felt compelled to retire because of Mr. Hubbard's verbal taunts regarding Li's age and Mr. Hubbard's unfair treatment regarding work, including shoving Mr. Li into the women's bathroom (Li Depo., 67:9-68:18; 233:6-234:8 Li Dec. pars. 5, 6, 9, 10,11). Ms. Pleitez also had work restrictions and Mr. Hubbard similarly ignored them. (Pleitez Depo., 137:20-139:17; 141:13-143:7; Hubbard Depo., 167:4-168:2; 174:19-176:4). Ms. Pleitez complained about the lack of an accommodation and was ignored. (Pleitez Depo., 183:22-184:16; 228:6-16). Mr. Hubbard stripped Ms. Pleitez of her permanent position in the emergency room and made her a floater because of her disability. (Hubbard Depo., 42:1-43:5). Mr. Hubbard told Ms. Pleitez that she was too old and should retire. (Pleitez Depo., 103:21-108:18). Defendant fails to show that this evidence cannot support the claims as a matter of law. Order signed in open Court.

Judge: Ernest Goldsmith. Clerk: Rosie Noguera. Reporter: Melanie Gheno, CSR #7489. (302/EG)
